s1_b1: Last time, you saw the planets slide into their orbits — down the bend in space. But look closer. Each planet keeps its own orbit, its own year. Mercury, close in, races. Neptune, far out, crawls.
s1_b2: Is that just chaos — every planet doing its own thing? Or is a planet's distance quietly setting how long its year is? There's only one way to find out. Measure.
s1_b3: It's not chaos. There's a rule — one clean rule, tying every planet's distance to its year. Find it, and one planet can tell you about another. Here's how.
s1_b4: Start with what you can actually measure. How far the planet sits from the sun. Call it r — the size of the orbit itself.
s1_b5: Then the time it takes to go all the way around, once. Call it T — the planet's year.
s1_b6: Now take two planets, not one. A and B. Not cryptic symbols — two worlds, held side by side. Earth and Mars.
s1_b7: That's what Kepler did, poring over Brahe's measurements. He wrote down r and T for planet after planet — and went hunting for the rule.
s1_b8: Try the ratio of the distances. r-A over r-B. How many times farther A sits than B.
s1_b9: And the ratio of the years. T-A over T-B. How many times longer A's year runs.
s1_b10: Cube the distances. Square the times. And the two sides come out exactly equal. That's the rule — Kepler's third law.
s1_b11: Why cubed, why squared? Double a planet's distance, and its year doesn't just double — it stretches further. Those exponents are exactly how much further.
s1_b12: So what's it for? Know how far a planet is, and you can predict its year — without ever timing it. Or measure its year, and pin down its distance. One world tells you another — not its mass, not the shape of its path, just how distance and time trade off.
s1_b13: Remember the bend. The farther out a planet sits, the gentler the slope, the slower it rolls. This equation is that bend — written in numbers.
s1_b14: No more weird codes. r is the orbit's width. T is the planet's year. A and B, two worlds. The exponents, the rhythm of the curve. Read it now — you can read it.
